coredata.dc:title: A comparative study of the carbon-based nanostructures for the delivery and detection of drug metronidazole
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinyu
Yang, Longyan
Sun, Xiyuan
coredata.dc:description: The interactions between the metronidazole molecule and three different carbon-based nanostructures, fullerene (C60), carbon nanotube (CNT), and carbon nanosheet (CNS), were examined using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The results indicate that C60 attaches metronidazole more stronger than CNT and CNS, and the adsorption energies of metronidazole with C60, CNT, and CNS are decreased after considering the solvent effects. The variation in energy gaps follows the order of CNS > C60 > CNT, indicating that CNS has better sensing performance for metronidazole compared to fullerene and CNT. Both CNT and CNS show a reasonable recovery time at an achievable operating temperature. According to the quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT), the dispersion component is the primary factor in stabilization, followed by electrostatic interaction. Simulated infrared (IR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra provide a basis reference for further structural characterization. This work indicates that carbon-based nanomaterials have potential applications in the delivery and detection of metronidazole.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanostructures
Metronidazole
DFT
Interaction
Drug detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structures of free carbon nanostructures and metronidazole
Adsorption structures and energies of metronidazole on nanostructures
Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of metronidazole molecule(a), C60 (b), CNT (c), and CNS (d).
The optimized molecule geometries of metronidazole@C60 (a1-a3), metronidazole@CNT (b1-b3), and metronidazole@CNS (c1-c3).
The density of states (DOS) in different complexes, metronidazole@C60 (a), metronidazole@CNT (b), metronidazole@CNS (c), and OPDOS (d).
Calculated molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) surfaces, metronidazole@C60 (a), metronidazole@CNT (b), metronidazole@CNS (c).
Molecular graph of metronidazole@C60 (a), metronidazole@CNT (b), metronidazole@CNS (c); the lines are bond paths.
The contribution of different energy terms to the stabilization, total SAPT0 interaction energy (TSAPT0), and the values for each component of SAPT.
IR spectra, metronidazole (a), metronidazole@C60 (b), metronidazole@CNT (c), metronidazole@CNS (d).
UV–visible spectra, metronidazole@C60 (a), metronidazole@CNT (b), and metronidazole@CNS (c).

The adsorption energy (E ads) of metronidazole molecule on the nanostructures in gas phase and aqueous solution, shortest adsorption distance (d) and charge transfers (CT) between molecules and nanostructures. Complexes (a1-a3) are the first three most stable adsorption structures of metronidazole@C60, b1-b3 are the first three most stable adsorption structures of metronidazole@CNT, and c1-c3 are are the first three most stable adsorption structures of metronidazole@CNS, respectively.
HOMO-LUMO gaps calculated with different functionals in conjunction with different basis sets for the nanostructures with and without metronidazole adsorption.
Recovery time (τ) of carbon nanostructures after adsorbing metronidazole.
Topological parameters (au) at BCP of interaction for metronidazole@C60 (a1), metronidazole@CNT (b1), metronidazole@CNS (c1).

Excited state, wavelength (λ), oscillator strength (f 0), and relative orbital contribution calculated of metronidazole@C60 (a1), metronidazole@CNT (b1), metronidazole@CNS (c1) in the gas and aqueous phases.

row.entry:
HOMO→LUMO+1 (34.0 %)
HOMO-1 → LUMO (25.2 %)
HOMO-1 → LUMO+10 (29.0 %)
HOMO-4 → LUMO+3 (15.6 %)

HOMO→LUMO+1 (27.5 %)
HOMO-1 → LUMO+2 (25.7 %)
HOMO→LUMO+10 (14.8 %)
HOMO-3 → LUMO+3 (14.5 %)
HOMO-2 → LUMO+4 (14.5 %)
HOMO-1 → LUMO+10 (13.5 %)

head.ce:title: A comparative study of the carbon-based nanostructures for the delivery and detection of drug metronidazole

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'an 625014, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Sichuan Agricultural University, Ya'’an 625014, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The interactions between the metronidazole molecule and three different carbon-based nanostructures, fullerene (C60), carbon nanotube (CNT), and carbon nanosheet (CNS), were examined using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The results indicate that C60 attaches metronidazole more stronger than CNT and CNS, and the adsorption energies of metronidazole with C60, CNT, and CNS are decreased after considering the solvent effects. The variation in energy gaps follows the order of CNS > C60 > CNT, indicating that CNS has better sensing performance for metronidazole compared to fullerene and CNT. Both CNT and CNS show a reasonable recovery time at an achievable operating temperature. According to the quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT), the dispersion component is the primary factor in stabilization, followed by electrostatic interaction. Simulated infrared (IR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra provide a basis reference for further structural characterization. This work indicates that carbon-based nanomaterials have potential applications in the delivery and detection of metronidazole.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole is a well-known antimicrobial drug derived from nitroimidazole azithromycin, produced by the genera Actinobacteria and Proteobacteria [1]. It is widely used to treat amebic infections, and rosacea acne, and to prevent infectious diseases caused by anaerobic bacteria and protozoa in various parts of the body [2–6]. However, misuse or overuse of metronidazole can lead to a series of toxic reactions such as spasticity, peripheral neuropathy, and ataxia [7]. Meanwhile, studies have also reported that excessive doses of metronidazole might be potentially carcinogenic [8], teratogenic [9], mutagenic, and genotoxic in humans [10]. Therefore, monitoring metronidazole concentration in patients undergoing antibiotic therapy is essential. However, current methods for detecting metronidazole such as spectrophotometry and chromatography have drawbacks including large instrument size, high cost, and being time-consuming.

In recent years, electrochemical methods have been widely used in environmental analysis and biological sample analysis due to their advantages of inexpensive instruments, fast response, and high sensitivity. Sensors constructed with nanomaterials offer fast response, high surface/volume ratio, and low cost, making them efficient electrochemical sensors [11–13]. Carbon nanostructures are considered as novel sensing tools for drug detection owing to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost as well as interesting electronic properties [14].
P.
Wu et al. developed a versatile approach for the biosensing platform of graphene by assembling enzymes/proteins on its surface as a means to prepare an efficient sensor for the detection of glucose [15]. M. H. Ghanbari and Z. Norouzi successfully designed an electrochemical sensor for the detection of adriamycin using a simple and low-cost method with high sensitivity and ease of use. They achieved this through nitrogen-doped carbon nano-ion-modified glassy carbon electrodes [16]. S. Hassanpour and J. Petr utilized single-walled carbon nanotubes modified screen-printed electrodes as disposable electrochemical sensors for rapidly quantifying dasatinib in pharmaceutical formulations [17].

Several researchers have applied density functional theory (DFT) to study drug delivery [18–20] and drug sensing [21,22] on nanostructures, which has significantly contributed to the use of computational materials science in designing and developing drug-delivering materials. G. J. Ogunwale et al. employed the DFT approach to explore the use of modified C60 fullerenes as sensors for detecting hydroxyurea (HU) and nitrosourea (NU) drugs [23].

In this study, the interactions of metronidazole molecules with three types of carbon nanostructures, including fullerene (C60), carbon nanotube (CNT), and carbon nanosheet (CNS), were examined, taking into account adsorption in both gas and solvent phases. The energy gaps (E g) between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LOMO) and partial density of states (PDOS) were calculated to understand the electronic structures. The bonding properties of metronidazole and the three types of carbon nanostructures were characterized using the quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) and symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT). The infrared (IR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectra of the complexes upon the adsorption of metronidazole were further analyzed.

The initial adsorption structure of metronidazole on nanostructures was obtained using the artificial bee colony algorithm [24] as implement in ABCluster 2.0 program [25]. Previous studies have demonstrated the successful use of ABCluster in generating cluster structures and its efficiency compared to other structure prediction software [26–29]. The initial structures obtained from the ABCluster were further optimized through DFT calculations using the ORCA program [30]. All calculations were performed by describing van der Waals (vdW) dispersion interactions using the hybrid PBE0 functional [31] and the def2-SVP basis set, as well as employing the DFT-D3 correction proposed by Grimme [32]. For the sake of comparison, the exchange–correlation functional of PBE [33] under generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and def2-TZVP basis set was also utilized. Molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) analysis was carried out using the Multiwfn program [34]. The adsorption energy (E ads) of the metronidazole molecule on the nanostructure surface was calculated using Eq. (1) [35]:
(1)
E ads
=
E complex
−
E nanostructure
+
E metronidazole

E complex is the total energy of metronidazole@nanostructures, while E nanostructure and E metronidazole denote the energy values of the individual nanostructures and metronidazole, respectively. A negative amount of adsorption energy corresponds to an exothermic process. The bond characters were analyzed using QTAIM calculations [36]. The Psi4 program [37] was used to perform energy decomposition calculations based on the SAPT [38] method. Interaction energies were calculated at the SAPT0/jun-cc-pVDZ level of theory, providing insight into the nature of the interaction between carbon nanostructures and the metronidazole molecule. Additionally, we predicted the infrared (IR) and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra of the studied complexes by frequency calculations and time-dependent density-functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations to further evaluate spectroscopic properties. The solvent model density (SMD) [39] was used with optimized structures to simulate the effects of aqueous environments on adsorption energy and UV–vis spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structures of free carbon nanostructures and metronidazole
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized metronidazole molecule and carbon nanostructures are shown in Fig. 1 . The lengths of the C1-N2 and N1-C2 bonds on the optimized five-membered heterocycle of metronidazole are 1.42 Å, 1.45 Å, and 1.52 Å for the C2-C3 bond, which is close to the values reported by Xu et al [40] The CC bond length in fullerene (C60) is about 1.45 Å between pentagons and 1.39 Å between hexagons, which is in agreement with experimental values [41]. The carbon nanotube (CNT) model is a zigzag (8,0) nanotube, with a length of 13.56 Å and a diameter of 6.31 Å, consisting of 96C atoms and 16H atoms (C96H16). There are two identified types of CC bonds in the CNT model: one related to the axis of the tube with a length of 1.43 Å, and the other diagonal with a length of 1.42 Å, which are consistent with previous reported value [42]. A nanocluster model containing 72 atoms (C54H18) was constructed to represent the carbon nanosheet (CNS) as shown in Fig. 1 (d). A similar cluster model has been successfully utilized in a previous theoretical study [43]. The optimized CC bond length between the hexagons in CNS is 1.42 Å, which is excellent in agreement with the theoretical results [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption structures and energies of metronidazole on nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The structural and electronic properties of the drug metronidazole when adsorbed on carbon nanostructures were examined. The most favorable adsorption position of metronidazole on the nanostructures was searched, and the most stable adsorption configuration is shown in Fig. 2 . The shortest bond length between the nanostructures and metronidazole is provided in Table 1 , along with the calculated adsorption energy.

The low E ads values suggest that metronidazole is physically attached to the nanostructures through non-covalent interactions. As can be seen from Table 1, the E ads value for the most stable adsorption configuration of metronidazole on C60 (a1 complex) is −0.470 eV in the gas phase, which is slightly more negative than the other two adsorption structures, −0.458 eV, −0.416 eV for a2 and a3, respectively. These adsorption energies are excellently comparable to those of C60 for other drugs (molecular weights in the 47–210 g/mol range) with a mean adsorption energy of −0.41 eV [46]. Negative E ads values indicate an exothermic reaction between metronidazole and C60. Additionally, it is observed from Table 1 that metronidazole is connected to C60 via a CN bond in the a1 complex with the shortest adsorption distance of 3.03 Å. When considering the aqueous environment, the E ads values of the complexes are all decreased compared to the gases phase. The CT value of 0.020 |e| in Table 1 indicates that the metronidazole molecule acts as an electron donor in the metronidazole@C60 complex.

Fig. 2 (b1-b3) illustrates three stable adsorption configurations of metronidazole molecule on the CNT surface with different orientations. The most favorable adsorption structure in energy is depicted as b1 in Fig. 2, in which the metronidazole was attached to CNT through a CO bond with the shortest adsorption distance of 3.04 Å. Additionally, the calculated E ads value for the optimized complex b1 was −0.700 eV in the gas phase and − 0.554 eV in the water solvent, both significantly stronger than the other two complexes, b2 and b3 (see Fig. 2) in the corresponding states. As indicated in Table 1, the CT value was 0.077 |e| corresponding to the transfer from metronidazole to the CNT.

In the gas phase, the E ads value of metronidazole@CNS (c1) is −0.934 eV, and the E ads values of c2 and c3 are −0.903 and − 0.897 eV, respectively, suggesting that the stability of c2 and c3 complexes is also considerable. In complex c1, the metronidazole was attached to CNS through a CO bond with the shortest adsorption distance of 3.04 Å. The predicted E ads value of the c1 complex in solvent is −0.774 eV, further indicating that the solvent causes a decrease in the adsorption energy. Although the E ads values of metronidazole@CNS are higher than those of metronidazole@C60 and metronidazole@CNT, the E ads value also indicates that the interaction between CNS and metronidazole is non-covalent in nature, leading to physisorption.

Fig. 3 shows the total density-of-states (TDOS) curves of the three most favorable configurations (a1, b1, and c1 in Fig. 2), along with the partial DOS (PDOS) curves contributed by the carbon nanostructure and the metronidazole fragments, and the overlap DOS (OPDOS) between them, where the HOMO level is shifted to 0 eV. In Fig. 3(a), it is evident that metronidazole contributes to the energy levels around HOMO, resulting in a certain change in the HOMO-LOMO energy gap after adsorption, as shown in Table 2 . Conversely, no significant contributions from metronidazole are observed for complexes b1 and c1, as illustrated in Fig. 3(b) and 3(c), indicating that metronidazole adsorption does not lead to remarkable changes in the energy gap. The PDOS curves also indicate a degree of orbital overlap between the carbon nanostructure and metronidazole. Fig. 3(d) shows the overlap population DOS (OPDOS) curves, in which complex c1 has the largest positive OPDOS value and the larger adsorption energy of the corresponding system, which is consistent with the results in Table 1. Additionally, Fig. 3(d) shows the overlap population DOS (OPDOS) curves, with complex c1 displaying the largest positive OPDOS value, which indicates the CNS attaches metronidazole more strongly, consistent with the results of E ads in Table 1.

The energy gaps (E g) between HOMO and LOMO obtained with different calculational methods are presented in Table 2. One can see that C60 shows a HOMO-LUMO (H-L) gap of 3.005 eV at the PBE0/def2-SVP level of theory, which is significantly larger than the experimental value (1.8 eV) [47]. When the PBE functional was employed, the calculated H-L gap of 1.701 eV (Table 2) was very close to the experiment. Previous works [48,49] have indicated that the calculated energy gap for C60 depends largely on the functional used. The hybrid PBE0 functional predicted the zigzag (8,0) CNT as a semiconductor with an H-L gap of 0.302 eV, which aligns with previously calculated results [50,51]. On the other hand, the PBE functional predicted the H-L gap to be close to zero since the pure functional generally underestimates the energy gaps. As for CNS, the energy gap calculated with PBE0 is 3.049 eV, close to previous results [52,53]. The GGA functional PBE further underestimated the H-L gap as 1.937 eV. From Table 2, one also found that both basis sets, def2-SVP and def2-TZVP in conjunction with the same functional obtain very similar H-L gap values, thus the influence of the basis set on the energy gap is fairly insignificant.

HOMO-LOMO gap (E g) is an important parameter for assessing conductivity (σ) characteristics. The conductivity (σ) is defined as follows [54],
(2)
σ ∝
exp
−
E g
/ 2
k B
T
where σ represents the conductivity, T is the temperature in Kelvin, k B is the Boltzmann constant. According to Eq. (2), σ is inversely related to the value of E g. The sensitivity (S) defined by the following formula [55,56],
(3)
S = ∣
σ pure
/
σ gas
− 1 ∣ = exp
Δ
E g
/ 2
k B
T
− 1

According to the above equation, a greater change in the E g value (ΔE g) indicates a stronger sensitivity (S). According to Table 2, a decrease in the E g value following molecular adsorption indicates an increase in the conductivity of carbon nanostructures after the adsorption of metronidazole. The order of change in the H-L gap (ΔE g) calculated at PBE0/def2-TZVP level of theory is CNS > C60 > CNT, with c1 (−0.063 eV) exhibiting a notably higher ΔE g compared to a1 (−0.043) and b1 (0.000). Furthermore, while the H-L gaps predicted by the PBE functional differ from those predicted by the PBE0 functional, they show the same ΔE g variation trend as the PBE0 functional. These results indicate that CNS is highly sensitive to the surface adsorption of metronidazole molecules, the effect of metronidazole on the electrical properties of C60 is weaker than that of CNS, and the effect on the electrical properties of CNT is almost negligible. As CNS can directly convert the presence of metronidazole molecules into an electrical signal, it can be used as a potential metronidazole detector.

Recovery time (τ) of a sensor refers to the time for this sensor to return to baseline value after the measured variable is removed. Adsorbents with short recovery times facilitate the development of efficient drug sensing devices. The recovery time (τ) is expressed by the conventional transition state theory as follows [57].
(4)
τ =
ν
− 1
exp
−
E ads
/
k B
T

Here, T represents the absolute temperature in Kelvin, k B is the Boltzmann constant, and ν is the attempt frequency. Adsorbate molecules can be recovered from adsorbents by exposing them to ultraviolet (UV) light. Under UV conditions, we calculate the recovery time of the favorable complexes a1, b1, and c1 using an attempt frequency ν = 1012 s−1 at different temperatures, and the results are shown in Table 3 . It is evident from Table 3 that as the temperature increases, the recovery time decreases. At the same temperature, the order of the recovery times of the three systems is C60 (a1) < CNT (b1) < CNS (c1). Also, the recovery time in the gas phase corresponding to a more negative adsorption energy is longer than in the aqueous solution. It is important to note that CNT shows a reasonable recovery time at room temperature, suggesting its practical applicability. The recovery time of CNS is also reasonable at 350 K, which is also an achievable operating temperature.

The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps display the uneven distribution of electronic charge in molecules, indicating areas of higher (positive charge) and lower (negative charge) electrostatic potential. MEP can be used to identify electron density, and sites prone to electrophilic and nucleophilic reactions. The MEP of the metronidazole-adsorbed carbon nanostructures was calculated and displayed in Fig. 4 . The variation in electrostatic potential on the molecule's surface was visualized using different color ranges, with positively charged regions shown in blue and negatively charged regions shown in red. As shown in Fig. 4, it is evident that the adsorption of metronidazole on C60, CNT, and CNS results in the charge transfer from the molecule to these nanostructures, which is in accordance with charge analysis results. The MEP analyses further reveal the dominant electrostatic interaction between metronidazole molecule and C60, CNT, and CNS nanostructures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum theory of atoms in molecules (QTAIM) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: QTAIM is commonly used to analyze the bonding characteristics based on the electron densities [43,58]. In this study, the interactions between the metronidazole drug molecule and three types of carbon nanostructures were analyzed to obtain more precise and reliable information. The topological paths identified between the C, H, O, and N atoms of the metronidazole molecule and the C atoms on the surface of the carbon nanostructures were depicted in Fig. 5 , and bond critical points (BCPs) associated with these atoms were found along these paths.

The topological parameters obtained from calculations at the interaction critical points, show the presence of BCPs in a complex and explain the nature of interactions between molecules. The values of electron density ρ(r), Laplacian of electron density (∇2 ρ), potential energy density V (r), kinetic energy density G (r), and energy density H (r) at the BCPs are summarized in Table 4 . According to a previous report [59], the values of ∇2 ρ and H (r) at BCPs can explain the interactions between molecules. When ∇2 ρ and H (r) are negative at BCPs (∇2 ρ < 0, H(r) < 0), the interaction is strongly covalent; when ∇2 ρ and H (r) are positive (∇2 ρ > 0, H(r) > 0), it is defined as a weak electrostatic interaction; and the partially covalent and partially electrostatic interactions are determined by ∇2 ρ > 0 and H (r) < 0. The analysis in Table 4 shows that the values of ∇2 ρ and H (r) are positive for all three complexes, indicating weakly electrostatic interactions. Furthermore, the ρ(r) values of the complexes are all lower than 0.01 a.u., further suggesting weak interactions. In addition, the -G (r)/V (r) ratio also reveals the nature of the interactions, −G (r)/V (r) > 1 indicates an electrostatic nature, while -G (r)/V (r) < 1 reflects a covalent nature [60]. From Table 4, it can be seen that all -G (r)/V (r) values are larger than 1, indicating a closed-shell nature.

The 0th-order symmetry adapted perturbation theory (SAPT0) method in conjunction with the jun-cc-pVDZ basis set was utilized to obtain the separate contributions to the total interaction energy (T SAPT0) between carbon-based nanostructures and metronidazole. As shown in Fig. 6 , the total interaction energy (T SAPT0) between metronidazole and C60 is predicted to be −12.44 kcal/mol, which is the lowest negative value among the three complexes studied. On the other hand, metronidazole@CNS (c1) exhibits the highest negative T SAPT0 value of −23.05 kcal/mol, indicating a stronger interaction between metronidazole and CNS. These results align with the adsorption energies. In SAPT0, the total interaction energy is decomposed into four components: electrostatic (E el), exchange (E ex), induction (E ind), and dispersion (E disp) energies. The symbols in subscript indicate the classical Coulombic interaction (el) between fragments, the exchange energy (ex) generated by the antisymmetrization of the wave-function, the induction term (ind) that results from polarization plus charge transfer between fragments, and the dispersion energy (disp) playing an attractive role. Typically, the E ex value is positive, and the other three terms are negative, collectively contributing to stabilization. The percentages of the E el, E ind, and E disp terms contributing to stabilization are also depicted in Fig. 6. It can be observed that the E disp term (around 70 %) predominantly contributes to attraction. The E el term contributes >20 % to stabilization, and the E ind contribution falls below 8 %.

The infrared (IR) spectra of the favorable complexes a1, b1, c1, as well as the metronidazole molecule were simulated using frequency calculations, and the results are presented in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the peaks of metronidazole at 1488.8 cm−1 and 1419.6 cm−1 are related to the mixed CC and CN stretching vibration. Additionally, the peaks at 1533.7 cm−1 and 1718.0 cm−1 correspond to the NO2 group of the asymmetric stretching vibration, and the peaks at 3060.2 cm−1 and 2989.0 cm−1 correspond to the CH stretching vibration of methyl and methylene groups. These IR peaks of metronidazole were also identified in previous work [61]. In complex a1, these IR peaks in adsorbed metronidazole are slightly red-shifted to 1485.0, 1714.4, and 3054.7 cm−1, respectively. In addition, the peak at 537.9 cm−1 is a characteristic peak of C60 corresponding to the bending vibration of CC, in agreement with that reported by Srivastava et al [62] The significant absorption peak of complex b1 at 833.6 cm−1 shows a bending vibrational mode relevant to edge CH bonds. The characteristic peaks of metronidazole are less pronounced but are red-shifted. Fig. 7 (d) shows that the absorption peaks at 1488.8 and 1718.0 cm−1 for metronidazole are red-shifted to 1484.5 and 1700.5 cm−1, respectively in complex c1. The absorption peak at 3215.5 cm−1 mainly comes from the symmetric stretching vibration of CH of CNS.

The UV–vis spectra of the studied complexes in both gas and solvent phases were obtained using time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations at the PBE0-D3 level of theory, considering 60 excited states. The important excited states, wavelengths (λ), and oscillator strengths (f 0) for the complexes in both gas and solvent phases are provided in Table 5 . Additionally, the molecular orbital isosurfaces relevant to the major transitions are depicted in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 (refer to supporting information). The UV–vis spectra of the favorable complexes a1, b1, and c1 are displayed in Fig. 8 .

In the gas phase, the absorption band for complex a1 appears around 340.5 nm, originating from S0 → S39 excitation. This transition is mainly attributed to the HOMO-3 → LUMO+3 (12.2 %) and HOMO → LUMO+3 (11.0 %) transitions. As shown in Fig. S1, both HOMO-3 and HOMO are constructed from the π orbitals of the carbon atom of C60. With the solvent effect taken into account, the absorption peak shifts to 359.6 nm, indicating the transition from S0 → S31. For complex b1, the absorption band located at 523.8 nm is due to the S0 → S41 excitation, predominantly a transition from HOMO to LUMO (22.8 %), which is mainly contributed by the p-orbitals of CNT. In the aqueous solution, the major absorption band of complex b1 is located at 525.6 nm, consistent with the gas phase results. The first absorption band at 264.0 nm of complex c1 is probably due to the HOMO-1 → LUMO+10 (29.0 %) transition. Referring to Fig. S1, all related molecular orbitals show π characters. The second absorption band of the complex c1 appears around 390.4 nm, which is identified as S0 → S6 excitations, with major contributions from HOMO → LUMO+1 (34.0 %) and HOMO-1 → LUMO (25.2 %). All related molecular orbitals show π characters. The apparent two absorption bands of complex c1 under solvent exhibit a negligible variation compared to those in the gas phase.

The interactions of metronidazole molecules with C60, CNT, and CNS nanostructures during drug delivery or drug sensing have been investigated using density functional theory calculations. The results indicate that these nanostructures attach metronidazole through physisorption. Among the three carbon nanostructures, CNS shows a more favorable affinity for the metronidazole than C60 and CNT. The binding interaction between CNS and metronidazole was found to be higher, as revealed by the OPDOS analysis. The adsorption ability of the nanostructures for metronidazole decreased to some extent when considering aqueous environment. Significant changes in HOMO-LUMO gaps were observed for CNS after metronidazole adsorption, indicating an increase in the electronic conductivity of CNS. The recovery times of these different carbon nanostructures were assessed, and the order of the recovery times is C60 < CNT < CNS at the same temperature. Notably, both CNT and CNS demonstrated a reasonable recovery time at an achievable operating temperature, suggesting its practical applicability. MEP analysis results showed significant charge transfers from metronidazole to the carbon nanostructures. The closed-shell interaction between metronidazole and the carbon nanostructures was revealed by the QTAIM. The SAPT calculations decomposed the interaction energy into the most important physical components suggested the dominance of the dispersion component inside the adsorption process along with the beneficial contribution of electrostatic and induction. Additionally, IR and UV–Vis spectra were simulated for the complexes, which will be useful for identifying the molecular structure in future experiments. This work will provide valuable guidance for the application of carbon-based nanomaterials in drug delivery and detection.
Xinyu Li: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Longyan Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Xiyuan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding support from the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (No. 2022NSFSC1824).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111562.
